name: Clark Terry
abstract: Clark Virgil Terry Jr. (December 14, 1920 – February 21, 2015) was an American swing and bebop trumpeter, a pioneer of the flugelhorn in jazz, and a composer and educator.

interviews.title: Clark Terry interviewed by Joe Williams and Michael Woods, Clinton, New York, May 19, 1995

quotes.text: Patterson and Jackson and all them from The Big Show. I don’t know if it was ‘51 or ‘52. But anyhow, I left and went with the band. I stayed with the band for almost ten years you know? And years later, I went up to the Carnegie Hall when Basie was already sick and he had to take a little side elevator to ride up, this was before they installed the thing that they’ve got there now. And I went backstage to see him and I’m standing at the top and he’s coming up and I said “you know one thing?” And he said, “yeah, so what’s that?” I said, “I have a confession to make to you, something that’s been bugging me for years and years.” He said, “yeah? what is it?” I said, “when I left the band you know, I told you I was sick and going home,” I said, “I wasn’t really sick.” He said, “um humm.” I said, “the reason I did that is because Duke had made me an offer I couldn’t pass up.” He said, “um humm.” He said, “why do you think I took the raise back, you think I didn’t know that?” All these years I’ve been bugged.
quotes.caption: Clark Terry recalls confronting Count Basie backstage at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Clark Terry interviewed by Joe Williams and Michael Woods, Clinton, New York, May 19, 1995

quotes.text: Huh? Yeah, the actress and dancer. We became like families. I had my wife come out to California for the first time, and we stayed at Gerald and Etta’s. I had a band to meet her at the airport. That was fun. So I’ve know Gerald for quite a long time. As a matter of fact, just very recently, we had an occasion to play the Berne festival in Berne, Switzerland. Gerald came over and performed Lunceford tunes with Jon Faddis’s Carnegie Hall band. Gerald and Snooky Young are the only two living members of the old Jimmie Lunceford days. [interruption of unknown length; the recording resumes in mid-sentence] . . . place to live for those who were travelers, close to the airport, right at LaGuardia, right on the Grand Central Parkway, Long Island Expressway, so ideally, whichever way you wanted to travel, it was convenient.
quotes.caption: Mentions Jon Faddis's Carnegie Hall band in conversation
quotes.doc_title: Terry Clark — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

quotes.text: Ray Copeland. Yeah, Ray played the Carnegie Hall concert with us.
quotes.caption: Terry recalls playing with Ray Copeland at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Terry Clark — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Junior Mance Part I
about_quotes.evidence: I’ve been working with another group called Lionel Hampton and the Golden Men of Jazz... usually it’s Al Grey on trombone, Clark Terry and Harry “Sweets” Edison on trumpets
about_quotes.doc_title: Junior Mance Part I interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, New York City, New York, July 27, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Lewis
about_quotes.evidence: We had Wardell Gray, Clark Terry, Gus Johnson on drums, Freddie Green on guitar, me on bass and like we used Bud DeFranco playing tenor.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Lewis interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, November 16, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Terri Lyne Carrington
about_quotes.evidence: Clark Terry gave me my first professional gig when I was ten, just as a special guest with him in Wichita, Kansas at the jazz festival
about_quotes.doc_title: Terri Lyne Carrington interviewed by Monk Rowe, Toronto, Canada, January 9, 2003

about_quotes.speaker: Charles Davis
about_quotes.evidence: I worked with Clark Terry... I was with his big band for a few years... I remember playing with Clark Terry’s band in Italy
about_quotes.doc_title: Charles Davis interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, August 23, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Mike Vax
about_quotes.evidence: Clark Terry... was a true mentor to me in so many ways... I played lead trumpet on his big band for the European tour
about_quotes.doc_title: Mike Vax interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, December 29, 2022

about_quotes.speaker: Schuller Gunther
about_quotes.evidence: Through all of my connections, I put together one of the greatest jazz orchestras that ever was. ... Clark Terry was.
about_quotes.doc_title: Schuller Gunther — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
